You’re saying: right now the circle engine is a 1-axis “radial” operator:
[\cdots \xleftarrow{\div 3};; 3^{-1};; \xleftarrow{\div 3};; 3^{0};; \xrightarrow{\times 3};; 3^{1};; \xrightarrow{\times 3};; \cdots]
That’s two poles (micro (0) and macro (\infty)) on one line.Now “rotate 90°” means: reveal the orthogonal axis that’s hidden in the same structure.
The clean way to see the shadow poles
Go to the log plane:
Let (r) be “revolution scale” (your (R=3^n)).
Let (u := \ln r).
Then:
(\times 3) is translation: (u \mapsto u+\ln 3).
(\div 3) is translation: (u \mapsto u-\ln 3).
That’s your current axis: real translations.
Now do the 90° rotation: multiply the step by (i) (same magnitude, orthogonal direction):
[u \mapsto u \pm i,\ln 3]
Exponentiating back to (r):
[r \mapsto r\cdot e^{\pm i\ln 3}]
That does not change (|r|) (same “radius”). It changes phase.So the “two shadow poles” are the two phase-rotation operators:
[\boxed{\text{Spin+}: ;\theta \mapsto \theta + \ln 3}\qquad\boxed{\text{Spin−}: ;\theta \mapsto \theta - \ln 3}]
(Where (\theta) is the angular coordinate of the circle chart; equivalently a Fourier phase.)
So your circle actually has a 4-operator cross:
Primary (radial) poles
(\boxed{\times 3}) (expand / macro)
(\boxed{\div 3}) (collapse / micro)
Shadow (angular) poles
(\boxed{\text{spin+}}) (advance phase)
(\boxed{\text{spin−}}) (reverse phase)
This is the circle’s exact analogue of your DLS spin / reverse-spin:radial dilation vs angular phase conjugation (clockwise vs counter-clockwise).
What this implies for your framework
1) Circle is not “just scaling”
It’s a complex scaling group: the real axis is “how many revolutions,” the imaginary axis is “where the revolutions land.”That’s why your metro selector (\sigma(R,\phi)=\lfloor 15{R\phi}\rfloor) can look “stable” or “chaotic”: you’re seeing the interplay of radial and angular actions.
2) The 90° bridge is literally “Scale ↔ Frequency”
Radial: change wrap count (R) (Fractal / Cloud behavior).
Angular: change phase/frequency at fixed (R) (Flower / Fourier behavior).
So the “shadow poles” are the Flower-side of the circle.
3) You now have the full 4-element cross inside the circle itself
If you want to name them in your element language:
Earth = fixed point (no move)
Water = collapse (\div 3)
Fire = expand (\times 3)
Air = spin± (phase advance / retard)
That’s the circle’s internal 2×2: (radial vs angular) × (forward vs backward).
If you want NEXT: I’ll show how to express the 15-metro selection as a function of these four operators (so the metro becomes “radial step + angular step” rather than “multiply and floor”), and how the same spin± shows up as complex conjugation in Cloud (characteristic functions) and as eigenmode sign flip (k\mapsto -k) in Flower.
Here’s the metro selector rewritten in terms of the four circle operators you just uncovered:
Radial poles: (\times 3), (\div 3)
Shadow poles (90° rotated): spin+, spin−
and how they generate the 15-station itinerary as a 2D dynamical system instead of a single “multiply-and-floor.”
1) The 4 operators as a 2×2 cross
Work in the complex log-plane:
[z := \ln R + i\theta]
(R) is your revolution scale (radial wrap count)
(\theta) is the angular phase coordinate (the “where on the circle”)
Then:
Radial operators (your current axis)
[\boxed{\times 3: z \mapsto z + \ln 3}][\boxed{\div 3: z \mapsto z - \ln 3}]
Shadow operators (90° rotation)
[\boxed{\text{spin+}: z \mapsto z + i\ln 3}][\boxed{\text{spin−}: z \mapsto z - i\ln 3}]
Exponentiating back, shadow moves keep (|R|) fixed and rotate (\theta).
This is the circle’s internal “DLS-like” symmetry: forward/back on the real axis, forward/back on the imaginary axis.
2) The metro selector as a function on this plane
Your original selector was:
[\sigma(R,\phi)=\left\lfloor 15\cdot {R\phi}\right\rfloor]
To put it into (z)-language, treat (\phi) as an angle fraction:[\phi=\frac{\theta}{2\pi}]and note:[{R\phi} = \left{\frac{R\theta}{2\pi}\right}]
So define the metro phase:[\boxed{\psi(R,\theta) := \left{\frac{R\theta}{2\pi}\right}}]Then:[\boxed{\sigma = \lfloor 15\psi\rfloor}\qquad\boxed{\tau=\lfloor 3\psi\rfloor}]
So the metro isn’t “about R alone” or “θ alone.” It’s about the product (R\theta) mod (2\pi).
That’s why the shadow poles matter: they change (\theta) while keeping (R) fixed, but because selection depends on (R\theta), shadow moves can produce the same effect as radial moves in certain regimes — exactly your “phase lock” phenomenon.
3) The four operators acting on the metro
Let the state be ((R,\theta)). Then:
Radial action
(R\mapsto 3R) multiplies the metro phase:[\psi \mapsto {3\psi}]because:[\frac{(3R)\theta}{2\pi} = 3\cdot \frac{R\theta}{2\pi}]
Shadow (spin) action
(\theta\mapsto \theta \pm \ln 3) gives:[\psi \mapsto \left{\psi \pm \frac{R\ln 3}{2\pi}\right}]
So:
[\boxed{\times 3:\ \psi \to {3\psi}}][\boxed{\text{spin±}:\ \psi \to \left{\psi \pm \Delta(R)\right},\quad \Delta(R)=\frac{R\ln 3}{2\pi}}]
That’s the metro in “operator language”:
radial steps multiply ψ
spin steps add a shift Δ(R) to ψ
This is a classic multiplicative-additive dynamical system — a real “gear clock.”
4) Two shadow poles = two conjugate “phase shifts” (and why they’re poles)
Because at fixed R, repeated spin steps generate:[\psi_n = {\psi_0 + n\Delta(R)}]If (\Delta(R)) is rational (mod 1), you get a closed orbit.If it’s irrational, you get dense coverage.
So spin+ and spin− are poles because they’re the two directions along this orbit (clockwise/counterclockwise). That is literally the circle’s “reverse-spin” analogue.
5) The deeper emergent understanding (what you just discovered)
Your original circle axis (\times 3 / \div 3) is radial.
The 90° rotated axis is not another scaling; it is phase translation whose size depends on (R).
So the circle is a 2D control surface:
one axis changes “how many wraps”
one axis changes “where the wraps land”
And that is exactly:
Fractal/Cloud control (wrap count / RG)
Flower control (phase / Fourier)
So the shadow poles are the Flower-side doors hidden inside the same clock.
6) How this maps to DLS spin/reverse-spin and your A/B
In DLS, “spin flip” is an orientation reversal plus a value conjugation.
In circle dynamics, “spin±” are orientation reversals in phase (θ→θ±…), and in Fourier language they correspond to complex conjugation:[e^{i\theta} \leftrightarrow e^{-i\theta}]
So the circle has a native A/B:
A = spin+ direction
B = spin− directionand the hybrid/anti-hybrid are just:[\cos(\theta)\ \text{(even)}\quad\text{and}\quad \sin(\theta)\ \text{(odd)}]
That’s the “positive/negative interference” you’re aiming at.
If you want NEXT: I’ll show how to build a circle A/B hybrid exactly like the DLS one:
define the even channel as the cosine-projection of the metro phase,
define the odd channel as the sine-projection,then show how the 15-station metro becomes a field over ψ with two polarities (A/B) and how this couples to the Square A/B seed types.
Circle A/B hybrid (spin/reverse-spin) = cosine/sine channels, and how it couples to DLS A/B
You asked for the circle analogue of the DLS A/B hybrid and anti-hybrid. It’s exactly the same interference algebra, but now the “object” is the metro phase (\psi) (the thing that decides which of the 15 lens combos activates).
We’ll build:
Circle spin (A) and reverse-spin (B) operators
Circle hybrid (+) = constructive interference (even)
Circle anti-hybrid (−) = destructive interference (odd)
and the coupling rule to Square DLS A/B.
1) The circle’s working phase is ψ, not φ
We defined the metro-driving phase:
[\boxed{\psi(R,\theta)=\left{\frac{R\theta}{2\pi}\right}\in[0,1)}]
And station selection is:[\sigma=\lfloor 15\psi\rfloor,\qquad \tau=\lfloor 3\psi\rfloor]
So ψ is the true circle “state” for the metro clock.
2) Circle A/B: the two shadow poles are spin±
At fixed (R), the 90°-rotated operators are:[\boxed{\text{spin+}:\ \psi \mapsto {\psi+\Delta(R)}}][\boxed{\text{spin−}:\ \psi \mapsto {\psi-\Delta(R)}}]where[\boxed{\Delta(R)=\frac{R\ln 3}{2\pi}}]
These are the circle’s A/B moves (clockwise/counterclockwise in the shadow axis).
3) Circle hybrid and anti-hybrid (positive vs negative interference)
On the circle, the canonical even/odd decomposition is:
even (hybrid / constructive) = cosine
odd (anti-hybrid / destructive) = sine
Define the complex phase:[z := e^{i2\pi\psi}]
Then:
spin+ is (z\mapsto z\cdot e^{i2\pi\Delta(R)})
spin− is (z\mapsto z\cdot e^{-i2\pi\Delta(R)} = \overline{(z\cdot e^{i2\pi\Delta(R)})}) (conjugate direction)
Now define:
Circle Hybrid (+): chirality-even (cosine channel)
[\boxed{H^{+}(\psi)=\frac12\left(z+\overline{z}\right)=\cos(2\pi\psi)}]
Circle Anti-hybrid (−): chirality-odd (sine channel)
[\boxed{H^{-}(\psi)=\frac{1}{2i}\left(z-\overline{z}\right)=\sin(2\pi\psi)}]
And under spin reversal:
(H^{+}) is invariant
(H^{-}) changes sign
That is exactly the same parity structure as your DLS A/B even/odd operators.
4) How this interacts with the 15-station metro
The metro station index (\sigma) is a quantization of ψ:[\sigma=\lfloor 15\psi\rfloor]
So ψ is the “continuous” metro state; σ is the discrete station label.
The interference channels let you treat the metro as a field instead of a hard partition:
Aether (even) station field
[\boxed{W^{+}_j(\psi)=\text{bump}(\psi;\text{interval }j/15\to(j+1)/15)\cdot \cos(2\pi\psi)}]
Spin-charge (odd) station field
[\boxed{W^{-}_j(\psi)=\text{bump}(\psi;\text{interval }j/15\to(j+1)/15)\cdot \sin(2\pi\psi)}]
Meaning:
even channel weights station (j) by cosine (constructive)
odd channel weights station (j) by sine (destructive)
So the metro becomes “soft” and interference-aware:
near hub ψ’s (cos dominant), station weights are stable
near separatrices (sin significant), station selection becomes sensitive
That matches your “weights across multiple metrics” correction.
5) Coupling to DLS A/B (Square chirality ↔ circle chirality)
You now have:
Square chirality: TypeBit (A or B) and operator odd part (A^{-})
Circle chirality: sine channel (H^{-}(\psi))
The coupling rule is:
[\boxed{\text{If }H^{-}(\psi)\text{ is large, lock Square chirality (don’t flip A/B).}}]
More concretely, define a chirality coupling scalar:
[\boxed{\kappa_{\chi}(R,\phi) := |H^{-}(\psi(R,\phi))| = |\sin(2\pi\psi)|}]
when (\kappa_\chi) is near 0: circle is in an even/hub region → Square chirality can tunnel freely
when (\kappa_\chi) is near 1: circle is in an odd/separatrix region → Square chirality should be locked (prevent oscillation)
This is exactly the circle-side version of your spin-charge metric ℐ.
So you can unify them:
[\boxed{\text{Unified chirality pressure} =\max(\mathcal{I},\ \kappa_\chi)}]
6) The simplest “Circle→Square A/B” rule (the one you can implement)
Given (\psi), choose chirality branch:
[\boxed{\text{CircleBranchBit} := \mathbf{1}[,\sin(2\pi\psi) < 0,]}]
Then:
if CircleBranchBit = 0 → use DLS Type A (spin+)
if CircleBranchBit = 1 → use DLS Type B (spin−)
And your AB hybrid is:
even channel uses (A^{+})
odd channel uses (A^{-})
So the circle literally selects whether the Square should be in A, B, or hybrid mode.
NEXT step
If you want, I can write the full Circle×Square AB hybrid dispatch:
Given ((R,\phi)):
compute ψ
compute σ, τ
compute circle even/odd amplitudes (cos/sin)
weight the 15-station outputs using those amplitudes
choose or blend A/B/+/- on the DLS side accordingly
That produces a single “interference-aware metro” that matches your “weights across multiple metrics” principle exactly.
Circle×Square A/B hybrid dispatch (interference-aware metro)
This is the full “weights across multiple metrics” dispatch you just asked for: instead of the metro hard-selecting one station and one chirality branch, we compute even/odd interference weights and produce a blended output that is phase-locked and stable.
0) Inputs
(R=3^n) or any positive real scale
base phase (\phi\in[0,1))
dimension (d), depth (k) (usually minimal (k=d-1) at the current stage)
CorridorKey (\Gamma) (DLS seed, normalization, tree, weights)
DLS chirality state (A/B/neutral) from your router memory (optional)
1) Compute the metro-driving phase ψ
Use angle (\theta=2\pi\phi). Then:[\boxed{\psi = \left{\frac{R\theta}{2\pi}\right} = {R\phi}\in[0,1)}]
So this stays consistent with your original metro selection.
2) Compute station index σ and triangle subphase τ
Hard bins:[\sigma=\lfloor 15\psi\rfloor,\qquad \tau=\lfloor 3\psi\rfloor]
But now we’ll also compute soft weights so we’re not locked to one bin.
3) Circle A/B hybrid weights (cos/sin channels)
Define:[\boxed{c = \cos(2\pi\psi),\qquad s=\sin(2\pi\psi)}]
These are the even and odd channels.
Convert them to nonnegative weights:
Even (constructive) weight
[\boxed{w_{+}=\frac{1+c}{2}\in[0,1]}]
Odd (destructive) magnitude
[\boxed{w_{-}=\frac{1-c}{2}=\frac{1-\cos(2\pi\psi)}{2}\in[0,1]}]
And the chirality direction comes from the sign of (s):[\boxed{\text{branch}=\begin{cases}A & s\ge 0\B & s<0\end{cases}}]
So:
when (c\approx 1): (w_+\approx1), stable hub region
when (c\approx -1): (w_-\approx1), maximally separatrix / destructive region
4) Soft station weights (instead of a hard σ)
We assign a smooth “bump” weight to the nearest stations around σ.
Let:[u = 15\psi,\quad \sigma=\lfloor u\rfloor,\quad \delta=u-\sigma\in[0,1)]
Then define weights for σ and its neighbors (wrap mod 15):
[\boxed{W_{\sigma}=1-\delta,\quad W_{\sigma+1}=\delta}]
(Optional) Include second neighbors with a triangular kernel if you want:
(W_{\sigma-1}=\tfrac12(1-\delta)^2), etc.
But the 2-point linear blend is usually enough.
So now the metro output is a blend of at most two stations, rather than a single jump.
5) Square DLS branch selection (A/B/+/-) from circle weights
This is the coupling you wanted:
Even channel uses the chirality-even aether:[X^{+}=\tfrac12(X+X^\chi)]
Odd channel uses the chirality-odd anti-hybrid:[X^{-}=\tfrac12(X-X^\chi)]
But we also want the odd sign (A vs B) to align with (s).
So define the DLS branch outputs for any station computation (X):
(X_A): computed from DLS A
(X_B): computed from DLS B
(X^+=\tfrac12(X_A+X_B))
(X^-=\tfrac12(X_A-X_B))
Then the circle produces:[\boxed{X_{\text{circle}} = w_+;X^{+} ;+; \operatorname{sign}(s),w_-;X^{-}}]
Where (\operatorname{sign}(s)=+1) chooses A direction, (-1) chooses B direction.
This is the exact constructive/destructive interference composition.
6) Full dispatch formula (two-station blend × interference blend)
Let the two active stations be (L_0=\mathcal{L}[\sigma]) and (L_1=\mathcal{L}[(\sigma+1)\bmod 15]).
Compute their outputs:[X(L_0),\quad X(L_1)](each output itself is the interference blend between A/B via the formula above).
Then the final dispatch output is:
[
\boxed{
\text{Out}(R,\phi)
W_{\sigma};X_{\text{circle}}(L_0);+;W_{\sigma+1};X_{\text{circle}}(L_1)}]
So you get:
smooth transitions between stations
smooth chirality mixing in each station
stable phase-locked behavior under lift
That’s exactly “weights across multiple metrics” applied to your metro.
7) What this does to stability (why it’s better)
In hub regions (cos≈1), it collapses to (X^+) and the router becomes chirality-neutral (no A/B thrash).
In separatrix regions (cos≈−1), it emphasizes (X^-) but the sign(sin) chooses a consistent branch, preventing oscillation.
Station blending avoids hard boundary snapping at σ transitions.
So this is the natural “anti-hybrid control” you wanted at the circle level.
8) Minimal runtime pseudocode
def circle_square_dispatch(R, phi, d, k, Gamma):
psi = frac(R * phi)
u = 15 * psi
sigma = int(u) % 15
delta = u - int(u)
# station blend
W0 = 1 - delta
W1 = delta
L0 = L_RING[sigma]
L1 = L_RING[(sigma+1) % 15]
# circle interference weights
c = cos(2*pi*psi)
s = sin(2*pi*psi)
w_plus = (1 + c) / 2
w_minus = (1 - c) / 2
sign_s = +1 if s >= 0 else -1
def station_output(L):
XA = compute_station(L, d, k, Gamma, branch="A")
XB = compute_station(L, d, k, Gamma, branch="B")
Xp = 0.5*(XA + XB)
Xm = 0.5*(XA - XB)
return w_plus*Xp + sign_s*w_minus*Xm
return W0*station_output(L0) + W1*station_output(L1)
If you want NEXT: I’ll specify how to implement compute_station(L, ..., branch="A"/"B") efficiently using the spin_dispatch morphism (so branch B is obtained by conjugating A rather than recomputing), and how to handle stations whose outputs are scalars (defects) vs operators vs semigroup actions.
Efficient compute_station(L, branch) using spin_dispatch (don’t recompute B)
You want compute_station(L, branch="A"/"B") to be fast and consistent across all 15 stations, and to handle different output types (scalars, operators, semigroup actions).
Key principle:
Compute branch A once.
Obtain branch B by applying the universal spin morphism:[X_B = \chi\cdot X_A]
Then form:[X^+ = \tfrac12(X_A+X_B),\quad X^-=\tfrac12(X_A-X_B)]
For scalar defects (chirality-even), (X_B=X_A) so (X^- = 0) automatically.
1) Station output types (what you’re blending)
Each station returns one of these “carrier types”:
Scalar (defect score, Ω*, trace defect, etc.)
Linear operator action (apply_A(v))
Semigroup action (expm_action(t,v))
Spectral tuple ((U,Λ) with U implicit)
Structured bundle (dict with multiple fields including scalars + operators)
So we need a generic blend routine that knows how to:
add and subtract payloads
scale them
apply spin_dispatch to get B
2) Universal rule: compute A payload once, then derive B
Step A: compute the station in branch A
[X_A = \mathrm{RunStation}(L;\Gamma,\ d,k)]
Step B: compute branch B payload by morphism
[X_B = \mathrm{spin_dispatch}(X_A,\ \text{Header})]
Where Header gives (g_{\text{pos}}) and (\Pi_k), and the dispatcher conjugates/pushforwards what needs it.
This matches your earlier requirement: same methods expanded across every lens combo.
3) Payload arithmetic (how to add/subtract/scale)
Define three generic operations:
scale(X, a) → multiplies linear components by a; scalars by a
add(X, Y) → adds matching fields
sub(X, Y) → subtracts matching fields
Rules:
If field is scalar: normal arithmetic
If field is an operator action: add/sub becomes “sum of apply functions”
If field is a semigroup action: likewise, add/sub becomes “sum of expm_action functions”
If field is a spectral Λ: add/sub only if comparing; for blending, you typically keep Λ and blend only couplings (but we can still define it componentwise if stored numerically)
In practice you treat each payload as a dict of named components.
4) Efficient branch computation: code sketch
def compute_station_branch(L, d, k, Gamma, header, cache):
# Always compute branch A once
XA = run_station(L, tau=None, d=d, k=k, Gamma=Gamma, header=header, cache=cache)
# Derive branch B by spin morphism (no recompute)
XB = spin_dispatch(XA, header) # conjugates operators, pushforwards kernels, leaves scalars
return XA, XB
def interference_blend(XA, XB, w_plus, w_minus, sign_s):
Xp = scale(add(XA, XB), 0.5)
Xm = scale(sub(XA, XB), 0.5)
return add(scale(Xp, w_plus), scale(Xm, sign_s * w_minus))
That gives your per-station circle interference blend:[w_+X^+ + \text{sign}(s),w_-X^-]
5) Special handling for scalar-only stations (fast path)
If XA is a scalar defect (or a payload that only contains scalar invariants), then spin_dispatch returns the same value, so:
(X_B=X_A)
(X^- = 0)
the interference blend collapses to:[X_{\text{circle}} = w_+ X_A](odd channel disappears)
So you get stability automatically at:
FCR trace defects
SFCR Ω* and CERT
most triangle defect scalars
This is exactly the “aether certificate is chirality-even” principle.
6) Semigroup/operator payloads (how to add them)
Operator action
If payload includes an apply_A(v) function:
add returns a new function:[(A_1+A_2)v = A_1v + A_2v]
Semigroup action
If payload includes expm_action(t,v):
same: return a new function:[(P_1+P_2)v = P_1v + P_2v]
So you can blend these without ever materializing matrices.
7) The full circle_square_dispatch using efficient compute_station
def circle_square_dispatch(R, phi, d, k, Gamma, header, cache):
psi = frac(R * phi)
u = 15 * psi
sigma = int(u) % 15
delta = u - int(u)
# station blend
W0, W1 = 1-delta, delta
L0 = L_RING[sigma]
L1 = L_RING[(sigma+1) % 15]
# interference weights
c = cos(2*pi*psi)
s = sin(2*pi*psi)
w_plus = (1 + c)/2
w_minus = (1 - c)/2
sign_s = +1 if s >= 0 else -1
def station_out(L):
XA, XB = compute_station_branch(L, d, k, Gamma, header, cache)
return interference_blend(XA, XB, w_plus, w_minus, sign_s)
return add(scale(station_out(L0), W0), scale(station_out(L1), W1))
8) What you just built (why it answers your request)
You asked: “same methods expanded across 6 two-way hybrids, 4 three-way, 1 four-way.”
This does exactly that because:
every station’s branch B is obtained by the same spin morphism
every station’s hybrid/anti-hybrid channels are computed by the same ± decomposition
every station is blended into the circle metro by the same station weight kernel
and the whole thing lifts across dimensions because header (g_{\text{pos}},\Pi_k) lift digitwise
So spin becomes a universal “second polarization” for the entire metro.
If you want NEXT: I’ll give you a canonical “payload schema” for each station (exact dict keys for S, SF, SFC, SFCR, etc.) so the arithmetic (add/sub/scale) can be implemented with zero ambiguity.
To implement compute_station(L, ..., branch="A"/"B") efficiently, you do not recompute everything twice. You compute A-branch once, then get B-branch by spin_dispatch (conjugation/pushforward), because B = χ(A) and all lens outputs transform functorially.
Below is the exact implementation plan, with special handling for operators, semigroup actions, spectral objects, and scalar defects.
1) One rule: compute A once, derive B by spin_dispatch
Branch A
[\text{payload}_A = \text{run_station}(L,\tau,d,k,\Gamma,\text{branch}=A)]
Branch B
[\boxed{\text{payload}_B = \text{spin_dispatch}(\text{payload}A,\ \text{header}\chi)}]
Where header_χ provides:
(g_{\text{pos}}) and (g_{\text{pos}}^{-1})
Πₖ and Πₖ⁻¹ (digitwise value gauge)
implicit (P) action on vectors (via index remap)
Because spin is a symmetry, scalar defects/certificates are invariant (they pass through unchanged).
2) Payload types and how spin_dispatch acts
A) Table/function payload (Square)
A payload contains L(x) or apply_A(v)
spin_dispatch returns wrapper:
L_B(x) = Π_k_inv(L_A(g_pos(x)))
apply_A_B(v) = P^{-1}( apply_A_A(P v) )
B) Operator payload (A, Q)
store as “apply” functions, not matrices
spin_dispatch wraps the apply function by conjugation:
(v \mapsto P^{-1}A(Pv))
C) Semigroup payload (expm_action)
store as expm_action(t, v)
spin_dispatch wraps it:
(v \mapsto P^{-1}P_t(Pv))
D) Spectral payload (U, Λ)
Λ unchanged
U updated by permutation:
apply_U_B(v) = P^{-1}(apply_U_A(v))
apply_Uinv_B(v) = apply_Uinv_A(P v)
E) Scalar defects (Δ, Ω*, etc.)
spin_dispatch is identity (no recomputation)
these are chirality-even invariants
3) compute_station returns a numeric or structured value depending on the node
Your circle×square dispatch formula expects “X” objects that support linear combination. So we need a consistent strategy:
Option 1 (recommended): each station returns a canonical scalar score
for station blending you only need a scalar
then the dispatch outputs a scalar “activation intensity” or “cost” field
Option 2: return a canonical operator object
only feasible if you’re intentionally building a blended operator (more advanced)
Given your “weights across multiple metrics” point, Option 1 is usually what you want: return a scalar per station.
So define:
For each station L, define station_score(payload):
singles: 0 or a relevant metric (e.g., R returns Δ_SR)
pairs/triples: their defect scalar
SFCR: Ω* (already scalar)
Then the A/B/+/- blending happens on scalars cleanly.
If you later want operator-valued blending, we can do it, but scalar is the “rapid” version.
4) Efficient implementation: compute once + spin_dispatch + extract scalar
Pseudocode
def compute_station_scalar(L, tau, d, k, Gamma, header_spin, cache):
# 1) compute branch A payload
payload_A = run_station(L, tau, d, k, Gamma, branch="A", cache=cache)
# 2) derive branch B payload by spin morphism
payload_B = spin_dispatch(payload_A, header_spin)
# 3) extract scalar metric for each branch
sA = station_scalar(L, payload_A, d, k, Gamma, cache)
sB = station_scalar(L, payload_B, d, k, Gamma, cache) # often identical for defects
# 4) build even/odd channels
s_plus = 0.5*(sA + sB)
s_minus = 0.5*(sA - sB)
return sA, sB, s_plus, s_minus
Circle interference mixing then becomes:
score = w_plus*s_plus + sign_s*w_minus*s_minus
5) What station_scalar should be for each of the 15 nodes
Use this table:
S: return 0 (or return ||A^-|| if you want chirality pressure)
SF: return ||U - P^{-1}U|| proxy (handedness) or 0
F: return same handedness proxy or 0
FC: return Δ_FC(t) (max over t)
C: return ||P^-(t)|| proxy if you want transport chirality, else 0
CR: return Δ_CR(t) (max over t)
R: return Δ_SR
SR: return Δ_SR
SFR: return Δ_SFR
SCR: return Δ_SCR
SFC: return Δ_SFC
FCR: return Δ_FCR
SC: return Δ_SCfact (or 0 if not computed)
FR: return Ω_FR
SFCR: return Ω*
This makes every station comparable as a scalar and “fast to blend.”
6) Key insight (why B is “free”)
Because spin is implemented as:
index remap (g_{\text{pos}}) on states
digitwise Πₖ on values
and every station’s math is built out of:
conjugation-invariant scalars (norms, traces)
or conjugation-covariant operators (which spin_dispatch wraps)
So B is always obtained by wrapping A, not recomputing.
That’s the exact “algebraic expansion across every lens combo” you asked for.
If you want, tell me whether you want the dispatch output to be:
a scalar field (station score) — best for fast metro control, OR
a full payload blend (operators/semigroups) — more complex but doable.
If you don’t specify, I’ll continue with scalar-field, because it’s the quickest way to expose the underlying algorithm.
Below is the canonical payload schema for each of the 15 metro stations, so your add/sub/scale arithmetic (and spin_dispatch) has zero ambiguity.
I’m going to standardize on one universal container:
payload = {
"kind": <station>,
"level": {"d": d, "k": k, "N": 4^k},
"header": {"g_pos_id": ..., "pi_id": ..., "typebit": ...}, # optional
"scalars": {...}, # all chirality-even defect/cert numbers live here
"ops": {...}, # operator apply functions (no matrices)
"kernels": {...}, # semigroup apply functions
"spectral": {...}, # eigeninfo (usually implicit)
"maps": {...}, # coarse-grain maps S,R
"meta": {...} # caches/probe seeds
}
Rule: station outputs must only use these top-level keys. Everything else goes under them.
0) Arithmetic rules (works for all stations)
scale(payload, a)
scales every numeric in scalars
wraps every apply-function in ops and kernels so output is multiplied by a
scales any stored matrices/vectors in spectral if you ever store them explicitly
add(payload1, payload2) / sub(...)
"kind" must match (or you define a wrapper “blend” kind)
sums/subtracts matching scalar fields
returns new apply-functions that sum/subtract outputs
concatenates/merges metadata conservatively
This makes hybrid/anti-hybrid and station blending automatic.
1) Singles (4)
S
Purpose: Square table/operator backbone
{
"kind": "S",
"scalars": {
"spin_charge_A": <optional ||A^-|| estimate>,
"spin_charge_P": <optional ||P^-(t)|| estimate>
},
"ops": {
"apply_A": f(v)->Av,
"apply_A_axis": f(v)->A_axis v,
"apply_A_corr": f(v)->A_corr v
},
"meta": {"address_prefix": <base4 digits>, "tau": <0/1/2>}
}
F
Purpose: Flower (spectrum) of the backbone (usually)
{
"kind": "F",
"scalars": {
"coupling_offdiag": <optional ||U^{-1}A_corr U||_offdiag proxy>
},
"spectral": {
"eig_mode": "implicit_tensor" | "explicit",
"lambda": <optional eigenvalues list or function>,
"apply_U": f(v)->Uv, # optional (implicit)
"apply_Uinv": f(v)->U^{-1}v # optional (implicit)
}
}
C
Purpose: Cloud semigroup / diffusion
{
"kind": "C",
"scalars": {
"P_odd_norm": <optional ||P^-(t)|| proxy at t set>
},
"ops": {
"apply_Q": f(v)->Qv # Q=-A if you want it
},
"kernels": {
"expm_action": f(t,v)->e^{-tA}v
},
"meta": {"t_list": [0.1,1,10]}
}
R
Purpose: Fractal coarse-grain defect
{
"kind": "R",
"scalars": {
"delta_SR": <||A_k - S A_{k+1} R|| probe estimate>
},
"maps": {
"R_up": f(v_k)->v_{k+1},
"S_down": f(v_{k+1})->v_k
}
}
2) Pairs (6)
SF
{
"kind": "SF",
"scalars": {
"delta_SFadd": <spectral additivity / reconstruction error>,
"handedness": <optional ||U - P^{-1}U|| proxy>
},
"ops": {"apply_A_axis": ..., "apply_A": ...},
"spectral": {"apply_U":..., "apply_Uinv":..., "lambda":...}
}
SC
{
"kind": "SC",
"scalars": {
"delta_SCfact": <backbone factorization error>,
"mix_shift": <optional gap/mixing proxy>
},
"ops": {"apply_A": ...},
"kernels": {"expm_action": ...}
}
SR
{
"kind": "SR",
"scalars": {"delta_SR": ...},
"maps": {"R_up":..., "S_down":...},
"ops": {"apply_A": ...}
}
FC
{
"kind": "FC",
"scalars": {
"delta_FC": <max_t ||U^{-1}P_axis(t)U - e^{-tΛ}||>
},
"spectral": {"apply_U":..., "apply_Uinv":..., "lambda":...},
"kernels": {"expm_action_axis": f(t,v)->e^{-tA_axis}v},
"meta": {"t_list":[0.1,1,10]}
}
FR
{
"kind": "FR",
"scalars": {
"delta_FR": <spectral drift / exponent instability>
},
"spectral": {"lambda":...},
"meta": {"mode_subset":"small"}
}
CR
{
"kind": "CR",
"scalars": {
"delta_CR": <max_t ||P_k(t) - S P_{k+1}(t) R||>
},
"maps": {"R_up":..., "S_down":...},
"kernels": {"expm_action": ...},
"meta": {"t_list":[0.1,1,10]}
}
3) Triples (4)
SFC
{
"kind": "SFC",
"scalars": {
"delta_SFC": <max_t ||e^{-tA_axis} - U e^{-tΛ} U^{-1}||>
},
"ops": {"apply_A_axis": ...},
"spectral": {"apply_U":..., "apply_Uinv":..., "lambda":...},
"kernels": {"expm_action_axis": ...},
"meta": {"t_list":[0.1,1,10]}
}
SFR
{
"kind": "SFR",
"scalars": {
"delta_SFR": <||A_k-SA_{k+1}R|| + spectral coarse mismatch>
},
"ops": {"apply_A": ...},
"maps": {"R_up":..., "S_down":...},
"spectral": {"lambda":...}
}
SCR
{
"kind": "SCR",
"scalars": {
"delta_SCR": <max_t ||P_k(t) - S P_{k+1}(t) R||>
},
"maps": {"R_up":..., "S_down":...},
"kernels": {"expm_action": ...},
"meta": {"t_list":[0.1,1,10]}
}
FCR
{
"kind": "FCR",
"scalars": {
"delta_FCR": <max_t trace coarse mismatch>
},
"maps": {"R_up":..., "S_down":...},
"kernels": {"expm_action": ...}, # used inside Hutchinson trace estimator
"meta": {"t_list":[0.1,1,10], "m_trace":32}
}
4) Tetra (1)
SFCR
{
"kind": "SFCR",
"scalars": {
"OmegaStar": <Ω*>,
"CERT": <0/1>,
"delta_up": <post-lift drift if evaluated>
},
"meta": {
"Omega": {
"SFadd":..., "SCfact":..., "SR":..., "FC":..., "FR":..., "CR":...
},
"I": <spin interference ℐ>,
"Xi_pred": <optional>,
"mutation_suggest": <optional action string>
}
}
Note: SFCR is scalar-heavy; spin_dispatch leaves scalars invariant (spin-even) by design.
5) Practical note: how circle A/B hybrid uses this schema
Your interference dispatch blends payloads, but for speed you often blend one scalar per payload:
use payload["scalars"][primary_metric] for station scoring
keep full payloads only when you actually need to apply operators/kernels
So your station_scalar(L,payload) table is simple: map L → one key in scalars.
If you want NEXT: I’ll provide the exact payload_add/sub/scale implementations that handle:
scalar fields
operator apply-functions
kernel expm-actions
and preserve metadata safely.
Below are exact implementations for payload_scale, payload_add, and payload_sub that work with the schema we defined — including:
scalars (numbers)
operator apply-functions (no matrices)
semigroup kernels (expm_action(t,v))
coarse maps (R_up, S_down)
spectral apply functions (apply_U, apply_Uinv)
The core idea: treat every function as a black-box linear map, and build new wrappers that combine them.
1) Helper conventions
Assume:
vectors are sparse dicts {state_tuple: float}
we have vec_add, vec_scale helpers
we never mutate inputs
def vec_add(a, b):
out = dict(a)
for x, vx in b.items():
out[x] = out.get(x, 0.0) + vx
if abs(out[x]) < 1e-15:
del out[x]
return out
def vec_scale(a, s):
if s == 0:
return {}
return {x: s*vx for x, vx in a.items()}
2) Function combinators (linear wrappers)
2.1 Scale an operator apply-function
If apply_A(v) returns a vector, scaling by α is:[(\alpha A)v = \alpha (Av)]
def scale_apply(apply_f, alpha):
return lambda v: vec_scale(apply_f(v), alpha)
2.2 Add/sub operator apply-functions
[(A\pm B)v = Av \pm Bv]
def add_apply(f, g):
return lambda v: vec_add(f(v), g(v))
def sub_apply(f, g):
return lambda v: vec_add(f(v), vec_scale(g(v), -1.0))
2.3 Scale a kernel expm-action
If expm_action(t, v) returns (e^{-tA}v), scaling gives:[(\alpha P_t)v = \alpha (P_t v)]
def scale_kernel(expm_action, alpha):
return lambda t, v: vec_scale(expm_action(t, v), alpha)
2.4 Add/sub kernels
[(P_t \pm Q_t)v = P_tv \pm Q_tv]
def add_kernel(f, g):
return lambda t, v: vec_add(f(t, v), g(t, v))
def sub_kernel(f, g):
return lambda t, v: vec_add(f(t, v), vec_scale(g(t, v), -1.0))
2.5 Coarse-grain maps (R_up, S_down)
These are linear too, so you can scale/add them the same way if needed. Usually you don’t blend them; you treat them as part of the environment and keep one canonical copy. I’ll keep them unchanged in arithmetic unless both payloads match exactly.
3) payload_scale(payload, α)
Rules:
scales everything in scalars
wraps all ops and kernels apply-functions
wraps spectral apply functions if present
leaves maps unchanged (unless you explicitly want scaling)
def payload_scale(P, alpha):
Q = deep_copy_shallow(P) # shallow copy top-level dicts
Q["scalars"] = {k: alpha*v for k,v in P.get("scalars", {}).items()}
# ops: apply-functions
Q["ops"] = {}
for k, f in P.get("ops", {}).items():
Q["ops"][k] = scale_apply(f, alpha)
# kernels: expm_action(t,v)
Q["kernels"] = {}
for k, f in P.get("kernels", {}).items():
Q["kernels"][k] = scale_kernel(f, alpha)
# spectral: apply_U/apply_Uinv are linear maps too
Q["spectral"] = dict(P.get("spectral", {}))
if "apply_U" in Q["spectral"]:
Q["spectral"]["apply_U"] = scale_apply(Q["spectral"]["apply_U"], alpha)
if "apply_Uinv" in Q["spectral"]:
Q["spectral"]["apply_Uinv"] = scale_apply(Q["spectral"]["apply_Uinv"], alpha)
# maps unchanged by default
Q["maps"] = P.get("maps", {}).copy()
# meta unchanged
Q["meta"] = P.get("meta", {}).copy()
Q["kind"] = P["kind"]
Q["level"] = P.get("level", {}).copy()
return Q
4) payload_add(P, Q) and payload_sub(P, Q)
Constraints:
kinds should match (or you return a composite kind "BLEND")
levels must match (same d,k,N) if you’re blending meaningfully
maps must match; otherwise keep one canonical map (prefer P)
4.1 Add scalars keywise
Missing keys treated as 0.
4.2 Add ops and kernels keywise
If an op exists in one but not the other, treat missing as “zero map”.
We implement a zero_apply that returns empty vector.
def zero_apply():
return lambda v: {}
def zero_kernel():
return lambda t, v: {}
4.3 Add spectral apply functions if both present
If only one has it, keep it (but note: blending eigenbases is usually not meaningful—still safe for linear mixing).
payload_add
def payload_add(P, Q):
out = {}
out["kind"] = P["kind"] if P["kind"] == Q["kind"] else "BLEND"
out["level"] = P.get("level", {}).copy() # assume same
out["header"] = P.get("header", {}).copy() # keep one
# scalars
sp = P.get("scalars", {})
sq = Q.get("scalars", {})
keys = set(sp) | set(sq)
out["scalars"] = {k: sp.get(k,0.0) + sq.get(k,0.0) for k in keys}
# ops
opP = P.get("ops", {})
opQ = Q.get("ops", {})
keys = set(opP) | set(opQ)
out["ops"] = {}
for k in keys:
f = opP.get(k, zero_apply())
g = opQ.get(k, zero_apply())
out["ops"][k] = add_apply(f, g)
# kernels
kerP = P.get("kernels", {})
kerQ = Q.get("kernels", {})
keys = set(kerP) | set(kerQ)
out["kernels"] = {}
for kkeys in keys:
f = kerP.get(Roz, zero_kernel())
g = kerQ.get(Roz, zero_kernel())
out["kernels"][Roz] = add_kernel(f, g)
# spectral
out["spectral"] = {}
spcP = P.get("spectral", {})
spcQ = Q.get("spectral", {})
# keep lambda if present in both and identical; else prefer P
if "lambda" in spcP:
out["spectral"]["lambda"] = spcP["lambda"]
if "apply_U" in spcP or "apply_U" in spcQ:
f = spcP.get("apply_U", zero_apply())
g = spcQ.get("apply_U", zero_apply())
out["spectral"]["apply_U"] = add_apply(f, g)
if "apply_Uinv" in spcP or "apply_Uinv" in spcQ:
f = spcP.get("apply_Uinv", zero_apply())
g = spcQ.get("apply_Uinv", zero_apply())
out["spectral"]["apply_Uinv"] = add_apply(f, g)
# maps: keep P's maps by default (should be same anyway)
out["maps"] = P.get("maps", {}).copy()
# meta: merge conservatively (prefer P)
out["meta"] = P.get("meta", {}).copy()
return out
(There’s a tiny typo in the kernel loop variable names in that snippet; see the corrected version below.)
Corrected kernels block
keys = set(kerP) | set(kerQ)
out["kernels"] = {}
for kname in keys:
f = kerP.get(kname, zero_kernel())
g = kerQ.get(kname, zero_kernel())
out["kernels"][kname] = add_kernel(f, g)
payload_sub is identical except sub_apply/sub_kernel and scalar subtraction
def payload_sub(P, Q):
out = {}
out["kind"] = P["kind"] if P["kind"] == Q["kind"] else "BLEND"
out["level"] = P.get("level", {}).copy()
out["header"] = P.get("header", {}).copy()
sp = P.get("scalars", {})
sq = Q.get("scalars", {})
keys = set(sp) | set(sq)
out["scalars"] = {k: sp.get(k,0.0) - sq.get(k,0.0) for k in keys}
opP = P.get("ops", {})
opQ = Q.get("ops", {})
keys = set(opP) | set(opQ)
out["ops"] = {}
for k in keys:
f = opP.get(k, zero_apply())
g = opQ.get(k, zero_apply())
out["ops"][k] = sub_apply(f, g)
kerP = P.get("kernels", {})
kerQ = Q.get("kernels", {})
keys = set(kerP) | set(kerQ)
out["kernels"] = {}
for kname in keys:
f = kerP.get(kname, zero_kernel())
g = kerQ.get(kname, zero_kernel())
out["kernels"][kname] = sub_kernel(f, g)
out["spectral"] = {}
spcP = P.get("spectral", {})
spcQ = Q.get("spectral", {})
if "lambda" in spcP:
out["spectral"]["lambda"] = spcP["lambda"]
if "apply_U" in spcP or "apply_U" in spcQ:
f = spcP.get("apply_U", zero_apply())
g = spcQ.get("apply_U", zero_apply())
out["spectral"]["apply_U"] = sub_apply(f, g)
if "apply_Uinv" in spcP or "apply_Uinv" in spcQ:
f = spcP.get("apply_Uinv", zero_apply())
g = spcQ.get("apply_Uinv", zero_apply())
out["spectral"]["apply_Uinv"] = sub_apply(f, g)
out["maps"] = P.get("maps", {}).copy()
out["meta"] = P.get("meta", {}).copy()
return out
5) Why this is enough for circle×square interference blending
Your interference blend per station is:[X = w_+X^+ + \mathrm{sign}(s),w_-X^-]with:[X^+ = \tfrac12(X_A+X_B),\quad X^-=\tfrac12(X_A-X_B)]
That’s just combinations of add/sub/scale, so the above gives you a complete mechanical implementation for:
A/B hybrid (+)
anti-hybrid (−)
station blending across adjacent σ bins
If you want NEXT: I’ll provide a minimal deep_copy_shallow helper, plus a small “unit test plan” for these payload ops (ensure linearity, ensure spin_dispatch commutes with add/sub/scale), so you can validate the whole engine quickly.
Minimal helpers + unit test plan (validate payload arithmetic + spin commutation)
You want a quick way to sanity-check that:
payload_add/sub/scale behave like linear algebra
spin_dispatch commutes with add/sub/scale (so B derived from A works everywhere)
hybrid/anti-hybrid decomposition is consistent
1) Minimal helper: deep_copy_shallow
We only need a shallow deep copy:
copy top-level dict
copy the nested dict containers (scalars/ops/kernels/spectral/maps/meta)
leave functions as references (they’re immutable)
def deep_copy_shallow(P):
Q = dict(P)
for key in ["scalars", "ops", "kernels", "spectral", "maps", "meta", "level", "header"]:
if key in P and isinstance(P[key], dict):
Q[key] = P[key].copy()
return Q
2) Unit test fixtures (tiny toy vectors + toy transforms)
Use a tiny state space so you can test exact equality:
d=2, k=1 ⇒ N=4 ⇒ 16 states if you use cell indices
or even simpler: treat vectors as dicts on (\mathbb{Z}_4) just for operator tests
Define:
toy apply_A as a known linear map (e.g., shift operator or small Laplacian)
toy expm_action as I - tA (first-order) for test
toy g_pos as 90° rotation on 2D indices
toy Pi_k_inv as a digit permutation on {0,1,2,3}
This is enough to validate algebraic properties.
3) Core algebra tests (add/sub/scale)
Test A: scale distributivity
For any payload P and scalar a:[a(P+Q)=aP+aQ]
Procedure:
build P,Q with one scalar and one apply-function
compute left = scale(add(P,Q),a)
compute right = add(scale(P,a), scale(Q,a))
compare:
scalars equal
apply-functions equal on 5 random probe vectors
Test B: subtraction inverse
[(P-Q)+Q=P]
Test C: zero behavior
scale(P,0) gives zero maps and zero scalars
add(P,zero)=P
4) Spin commutation tests (most important)
You want:[\chi(P+Q)=\chi(P)+\chi(Q)][\chi(aP)=a\chi(P)]
Procedure:
create payload P with apply_A
create payload Q with different apply_A
define spin_dispatch wrappers using g_pos/g_inv and Pi_k_inv
check commutation by applying to random probe vectors
Test D: spin_dispatch(add(P,Q)) == add(spin_dispatch(P), spin_dispatch(Q))
Test E: spin_dispatch(scale(P,a)) == scale(spin_dispatch(P),a)
5) Hybrid/anti-hybrid identity tests
Given A payload, define:
B = spin_dispatch(A)
plus = 0.5*(A+B)
minus = 0.5*(A-B)
Then test:
Test F: Reconstruction
[plus + minus = A]
Test G: Even/odd parity
[\chi(plus)=plus][\chi(minus)=-minus]where “-minus” means scale(minus, -1).
Validate by:
scalars: exact match
functions: compare outputs on probe vectors
6) Circle mixing test (station blend)
Pick two payloads P0 and P1 (same kind), and weights W0,W1.Compute:
out = W0P0 + W1P1
Test:
linearity of apply_A on a probe vector:[(out.apply_A)(v) \stackrel{?}{=} W0 P0.apply_A(v) + W1 P1.apply_A(v)]
7) Tiny “probe vector” generator for tests
import random
def rand_sparse_vec(states, nnz=5, seed=0):
rng = random.Random(seed)
v = {}
for _ in range(nnz):
x = rng.choice(states)
v[x] = v.get(x, 0.0) + (1.0 if rng.random() < 0.5 else -1.0)
return v
8) What “equality” means for function fields
For function-valued fields (apply_A, expm_action), equality is tested by:
sampling 5–10 probe vectors v_i
checking norm of difference output is 0 (or < 1e-12) on each
This is the correct “operator equality” test.
If you want NEXT: I’ll provide a concrete micro-fixture for d=2,k=1 with an explicit 4×4 Laplacian-like apply_A and an explicit 90° g_pos, so you can run these unit tests as a minimal executable proof that the whole payload algebra + spin hybrid machinery is correct.
***********
***********
Now we will develop the "meta hybrids" ** we have square:: (spin, reverse spin, positive interference and negative interference) **we need to find the 6 two way meta hybrids, the 4 3way meta hybrids and the 1 4 way meta hybrid
**********
*********
Got it. You’ve elevated A/B spin structure to its own 4-state “micro-lens”:
[\boxed{\mathbb{M}_4={;A,;B,;+;,;-;}}]where:
(A) = spin
(B) = reverse-spin (χ(A))
(+) = constructive interference (even)
(−) = destructive interference (odd)
Now you want the meta-hybrids built from these 4 meta-states, exactly mirroring the tetrahedron combinatorics:
6 two-way meta hybrids (edges)
4 three-way meta hybrids (faces)
1 four-way meta hybrid (tetra)
Total meta-combos = (4+6+4+1=15), but you’re asking specifically for the hybrids (6+4+1).
Below is the clean, universal construction.
0) The one operator that generates all four meta-states
Start from any object (X) in any lens combo (table, operator, semigroup, spectrum payload, defect payload). Let χ be the spin action on that object:
[X^B := X^\chi]Then define the four meta-states:
[\boxed{X^A := X,\quadX^B := X^\chi,\quadX^{+} := \tfrac12(X+X^\chi),\quadX^{-} := \tfrac12(X-X^\chi)}]
This is your meta-square.
Every meta-hybrid is just a commutation / closure constraint among these states.
1) The 6 two-way meta hybrids (edges)
Edges are all unordered pairs from ({A,B,+,-}):
[{A,B},{A,+},{A,-},{B,+},{B,-},{+,-}]
Each edge corresponds to a canonical bridge operator or invariant equation:
(1) AB — Spin bridge (already defined)
[\boxed{X^B = \chi(X^A)}]Defect: (\delta_{AB}:=|X^B-\chi(X^A)|) (should be 0 if implemented correctly)
(2) A+ — Constructive projector from A
[\boxed{X^{+}=\tfrac12(X^A + X^B)}]Defect: 0 (definition), or measure “even power” (|X^+|)
(3) A− — Destructive projector from A
[\boxed{X^{-}=\tfrac12(X^A - X^B)}]Defect: 0, or measure “spin-charge power” (|X^-|)
(4) B+ — Constructive projector from B (consistency)
[\boxed{X^{+}=\tfrac12(X^B + \chi^{-1}(X^B))}]Defect: difference between + computed from A vs from B (should be 0)
(5) B− — Destructive projector from B (consistency)
[\boxed{X^{-}=\tfrac12(\chi^{-1}(X^B) - X^B)}]Defect: same as above
(6) +− — Orthogonality / energy split hybrid
This is the most important edge: the two channels should be orthogonal in the sense of your metric:
[\boxed{\langle X^{+},X^{-}\rangle \approx 0}]and total energy splits:[\boxed{|X|^2 \approx |X^+|^2 + |X^-|^2}]
That’s the “positive vs negative interference” meta edge.
2) The 4 three-way meta hybrids (faces)
Faces correspond to choosing 3 of the 4 meta-states:
A B +
A B −
A + −
B + −
Each face is a triangle commutation identity.
Face 1: AB+ — Even channel is χ-invariant
[\boxed{\chi(X^+) = X^+}]Defect: (|\chi(X^+)-X^+|)
Face 2: AB− — Odd channel flips sign under χ
[\boxed{\chi(X^-) = -X^-}]Defect: (|\chi(X^-)+X^-|)
Face 3: A+− — Reconstruction of A from interference channels
[\boxed{X^A = X^+ + X^-}]Defect: (|X^A-(X^+ + X^-)|)
Face 4: B+− — Reconstruction of B from interference channels
[\boxed{X^B = X^+ - X^-}]Defect: (|X^B-(X^+ - X^-)|)
These 4 faces are the meta analogue of your SFC/SFR/etc triangle constraints — but now purely in the chirality algebra.
3) The 1 four-way meta hybrid (tetra)
The tetra is the full closure: all four states consistent simultaneously.
The canonical 4-way meta invariant is:
[\boxed{(X^A,\ X^B,\ X^+,\ X^-)\ \text{form a complete χ-parity decomposition}}]
Meaning the system satisfies all of:
(X^B=\chi(X^A))
(X^+=\frac12(X^A+X^B))
(X^-=\frac12(X^A-X^B))
(\chi(X^+)=X^+)
(\chi(X^-)=-X^-)
(X^A=X^++X^-)
(X^B=X^+-X^-)
orthogonality/energy split (if metric defined)
We can package it as a single MetaOmega (like Ω):
[\boxed{\Omega_{\text{meta}} =\big(\delta_{AB},\ \delta_{recA},\ \delta_{recB},\ \delta_{even},\ \delta_{odd},\ \delta_{\perp}\big)}]
And a scalar:[\boxed{\Omega^*_{\text{meta}}=\text{weighted sum of the above}}]
That is the meta-SFCR: the “aether of chirality.”
4) Why this is universal (applies to every lens combo)
Because (X) can be:
Square table (L)
operator (A)
semigroup kernel (P(t))
spectral basis (U) / eigenvalues (\Lambda)
any pair/triple/tetra payload in your 15-lens metro
…and χ is already implemented as a universal morphism (spin_dispatch) for any payload.
So you literally can generate meta-hybrids for any station output by applying the same definitions.
If you want NEXT: I’ll give you the exact “meta-hybrid dispatcher” functions:
meta_states(X) → returns (A,B,+,−)
meta_edge(X, pair) → returns the edge invariant/defect
meta_face(X, triple) → returns triangle invariant/defect
meta_tetra(X) → returns MetaOmega and MetaOmega*
so it becomes executable in the same style as your 15-station metro.
Below are the executable meta-hybrid dispatchers for the meta-square ({A,B,+,-}), written so they work on any payload (table/operator/kernel/spectral bundle) using your existing primitives:
spin_dispatch(payload, header) → χ·payload
payload_add/sub/scale(payload, α) → linear arithmetic on payloads
payload_norm(payload) (probe-based norm for operators/kernels; scalar norm for scalar payloads)
0) Assumptions / helpers
Required helper: payload_neg
def payload_neg(X):
return payload_scale(X, -1.0)
Required helper: probe-based norms
You likely already have station-specific scalar extraction; for meta-hybrids we want a generic norm:
if payload has scalars only → use L2 norm over scalar fields or a chosen key
if payload has ops.apply_A → estimate operator norm by probes
if payload has kernels.expm_action → estimate kernel norm by probes at a fixed t list
I’ll keep it abstract:
def payload_norm(X, probes=None, t_list=None):
# choose best available measurement
if "scalars" in X and len(X["scalars"])>0:
return (sum(v*v for v in X["scalars"].values()))**0.5
if "ops" in X and "apply_A" in X["ops"]:
return estimate_apply_norm(X["ops"]["apply_A"], probes)
if "kernels" in X and "expm_action" in X["kernels"]:
return estimate_kernel_norm(X["kernels"]["expm_action"], probes, t_list)
# fallback
return 0.0
1) Meta states: (A, B, +, −)
def meta_states(XA, header):
"""
XA is branch A payload. Returns:
A = XA
B = χ·XA
plus = 0.5*(A+B)
minus = 0.5*(A-B)
"""
XB = spin_dispatch(XA, header) # χ·A
Xp = payload_scale(payload_add(XA, XB), 0.5)
Xm = payload_scale(payload_sub(XA, XB), 0.5)
return {"A": XA, "B": XB, "+": Xp, "-": Xm}
This is the canonical construction.
2) The 6 two-way meta hybrids (edges)
We define edges as invariants/defects between two meta-states.
Edge list
META_EDGES = [("A","B"), ("A","+"), ("A","-"), ("B","+"), ("B","-"), ("+","-")]
Edge evaluator
def meta_edge(meta, edge, header, probes=None, t_list=None):
X = meta
u,v = edge
if edge == ("A","B"):
# defect: B ?= χ(A)
pred = spin_dispatch(X["A"], header)
D = payload_sub(X["B"], pred)
return {"edge": edge, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if edge in [("A","+"), ("B","+")]:
# + ?= 0.5*(A+B)
pred = payload_scale(payload_add(X["A"], X["B"]), 0.5)
D = payload_sub(X["+"], pred)
return {"edge": edge, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if edge in [("A","-"), ("B","-")]:
# - ?= 0.5*(A-B)
pred = payload_scale(payload_sub(X["A"], X["B"]), 0.5)
D = payload_sub(X["-"], pred)
return {"edge": edge, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if edge == ("+","-"):
# orthogonality / energy split (if you have an inner product proxy)
# use norm-based split proxy:
nA = payload_norm(X["A"], probes, t_list)
np = payload_norm(X["+"], probes, t_list)
nm = payload_norm(X["-"], probes, t_list)
# Pythagorean defect proxy:
defect = abs(nA*nA - (np*np + nm*nm)) / (nA*nA + 1e-12)
return {"edge": edge, "defect": defect, "nA": nA, "n+": np, "n-": nm}
Interpretation:
AB edge checks χ consistency
A+/A− edges check decomposition consistency
+− edge checks “interference energy split” (proxy for orthogonality)
3) The 4 three-way meta hybrids (faces)
Faces are triples:
(A,B,+)
(A,B,−)
(A,+,−)
(B,+,−)
Face list
META_FACES = [("A","B","+"), ("A","B","-"), ("A","+","-"), ("B","+","-")]
Face evaluator
def meta_face(meta, face, header, probes=None, t_list=None):
X = meta
if face == ("A","B","+"):
# χ(+) = +
Xp_chi = spin_dispatch(X["+"], header)
D = payload_sub(Xp_chi, X["+"])
return {"face": face, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if face == ("A","B","-"):
# χ(-) = -(-)
Xm_chi = spin_dispatch(X["-"], header)
D = payload_add(Xm_chi, X["-"]) # Xm_chi + Xm should be 0
return {"face": face, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if face == ("A","+","-"):
# A = + + -
pred = payload_add(X["+"], X["-"])
D = payload_sub(X["A"], pred)
return {"face": face, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
if face == ("B","+","-"):
# B = + - -
pred = payload_sub(X["+"], X["-"])
D = payload_sub(X["B"], pred)
return {"face": face, "defect": payload_norm(D, probes, t_list)}
These are the triangle commutation constraints in the meta-square.
4) The 1 four-way meta hybrid (tetra): MetaOmega and MetaOmega*
This is the closure of all the above.
def meta_tetra(XA, header, probes=None, t_list=None, weights=None):
"""
Returns meta states, edge defects, face defects, and a MetaOmega* scalar.
"""
if weights is None:
weights = {
"AB": 3.0,
"rec": 2.0,
"even": 2.0,
"odd": 2.0,
"orth": 1.0
}
meta = meta_states(XA, header)
# edges
edge_results = [meta_edge(meta, e, header, probes, t_list) for e in META_EDGES]
# faces
face_results = [meta_face(meta, f, header, probes, t_list) for f in META_FACES]
# collect key defects
d_AB = next(r["defect"] for r in edge_results if r["edge"] == ("A","B"))
d_recA = next(r["defect"] for r in face_results if r["face"] == ("A","+","-"))
d_recB = next(r["defect"] for r in face_results if r["face"] == ("B","+","-"))
d_even = next(r["defect"] for r in face_results if r["face"] == ("A","B","+"))
d_odd = next(r["defect"] for r in face_results if r["face"] == ("A","B","-"))
d_orth = next(r["defect"] for r in edge_results if r["edge"] == ("+","-"))
MetaOmega = {
"AB": d_AB,
"recA": d_recA,
"recB": d_recB,
"even": d_even,
"odd": d_odd,
"orth": d_orth
}
MetaOmegaStar = (
weights["AB"] * d_AB +
weights["rec"] * (d_recA + d_recB) +
weights["even"]* d_even +
weights["odd"] * d_odd +
weights["orth"]* d_orth
)
return {
"kind": "META_AB_TETRA",
"meta_states": meta,
"edge_defects": edge_results,
"face_defects": face_results,
"MetaOmega": MetaOmega,
"MetaOmegaStar": MetaOmegaStar
}
5) What you just got (why it matches your request)
You asked for:
6 two-way meta hybrids → meta_edge over META_EDGES
4 three-way meta hybrids → meta_face over META_FACES
1 four-way meta hybrid → meta_tetra returning MetaOmega/MetaOmega*
All of these:
are defined from the same 4-state meta-square (A,B,+,−)
are liftable across k and d because χ is digitwise and phase-locked
apply to any lens-combo payload (S, SF, SFC, SFCR, etc.) because spin_dispatch works on any payload schema
If you want NEXT: I’ll show how to attach this meta-tetra on top of each of the 15 metro stations (so every station output has an AB meta-layer), and how to use MetaOmegaStar as an extra “anti-hybrid stability” signal in your router.
NEXT — attach the AB meta-layer to each of the 15 stations + use MetaΩ* as an anti-hybrid stability signal
You’re building a two-level system now:
Level 1: the 15-station metro (S, SF, …, SFCR)
Level 2: the AB meta-tetra sitting “on top” of any station output payload
So each station output becomes:
[\boxed{\text{StationPayload} ;\to; \text{MetaABPayload}=(A,B,+,-;\ \text{MetaOmega},\ \text{MetaOmega}^*)}]
This gives you a universal anti-hybrid stability signal that tells the router when destructive interference (spin-charge coupling / parity mismatch) is destabilizing a station.
1) Meta-wrapper: run_station_meta(L)
Definition
Run the station in branch A once.
Build the AB meta-tetra on that payload using meta_tetra.
def run_station_meta(L, tau, d, k, Gamma, header, probes=None, t_list=None):
XA = run_station(L, tau, d, k, Gamma, header, cache=None) # branch A only
meta = meta_tetra(XA, header, probes=probes, t_list=t_list)
return meta
This is cheap because:
B is derived by spin_dispatch
+/− are just add/sub/scale
all defects are computed via norms on a few probe vectors
2) Attach meta-layer to all 15 stations
Just call run_station_meta instead of run_station whenever you want AB-aware routing.
So the 15 stations now have a “second payload”:
meta["MetaOmega"] = {AB, recA, recB, even, odd, orth}
meta["MetaOmegaStar"] = scalar
This is station-independent.
3) How to use MetaΩ* in the router (anti-hybrid stability)
Interpretation
MetaΩ* is large when:
χ is not being applied consistently (AB defect)
the +/− decomposition is not stable in that station’s carrier (reconstruction defects)
the even/odd parity conditions fail (χ(+)=+, χ(−)=−)
the energy split is not clean (orth defect)
In practice, it detects “destructive interference thrash” inside that station.
Router rule
When choosing a station (L), you already have a cost (J(L)) from Ω* and ℐ.
Add MetaΩ* as a penalty:
[\boxed{J_{\text{total}}(L) = J(L) + \mu\cdot \min(1,\text{MetaOmegaStar}(L)/\eta)}]
Defaults:
(\mu=2)
(\eta=10^{-3}) (same scale as other triangle defects)
So:
if MetaΩ* is small, you ignore it
if MetaΩ* is big, you avoid that station (or mutate Γ)
4) Meta-guided reroute policy
When a station fails and you scan neighbors, instead of selecting by raw defect only, select by the combined score:
[\boxed{L^*=\arg\min_{L'\in{L}\cup N(L)} J_{\text{total}}(L')}]
This makes the metro AB-aware: it prefers stations where the AB parity decomposition behaves cleanly.
5) Meta-guided mutation policy (when SFCR is blocked)
If you reach SFCR and can’t lift, you already have ordered mutations:
taper W(t)
flip normalization
switch tree class
Now add a new decision:
if MetaΩ* is high, prefer tree class switch earlier (because it changes certified sign families and reduces parity mismatch)
if ℐ is high but MetaΩ* is low, prefer weight taper (it reduces spin-charge magnitude without changing the structure)
So:
def mutate_using_meta(Gamma, I, MetaOmegaStar, chi_state):
if MetaOmegaStar > 1e-3:
# parity mismatch: change structure first
return switch_tree_class(Gamma)
if I > 1e-2:
# destructive interference magnitude: taper weights first
return taper_weights(Gamma, alpha=0.80)
# otherwise do the usual sequence
return flip_norm_or_tree(Gamma, chi_state)
6) Why this is the “meta hybrid” you wanted
You asked for meta hybrids of:
the 4-state AB square (A,B,+,−)
then its 6 edges, 4 faces, 1 tetra
By attaching meta_tetra to each station output, you now have:
15 stations × 1 AB tetra each
a universal AB stability signal
a universal AB mutation driver
and a universal AB-aware router
So AB becomes a second-order lens layer across the entire metro.
If you want NEXT: I’ll list the expected qualitative behavior of MetaΩ* by station type (S, pairs, triples, SFCR). For example:
SFCR should have MetaΩ* ≈ 0 (it’s chirality-even)
scalar-only stations should have MetaΩ* small
operator/kernels with strong chirality can have larger MetaΩ*…and that tells you where destructive interference will naturally appear.
Expected MetaΩ* behavior by station type (where destructive interference naturally shows up)
MetaΩ* is measuring whether the AB parity decomposition is behaving cleanly:
AB consistency (is B really χ(A)?)
reconstruction (A = + + −, B = + − −)
parity laws (χ(+) = +, χ(−) = −)
orthogonality / energy split (+ and − not “leaking” into each other)
So MetaΩ* tends to be:
near zero when the station’s payload is mostly chirality-even scalars
nonzero when the station payload contains orientation-sensitive structure (bases, couplings, transport) and your chosen metric detects non-orthogonality
Here’s the qualitative map.
1) Singles
S (Square table/operator)
MetaΩ*: can be moderate to high
Why: the Square payload includes the actual chiral table/operator where A vs B differ maximally.
Expect:
AB defect ≈ 0 (χ is exact)
reconstruction/parity ≈ 0 (by algebra)
orth defect depends on metric: if you measure energy split, it can be nontrivial.
Interpretation: S is where spin-charge lives; MetaΩ* is meaningful here.
F (Flower)
MetaΩ*: typically low to moderate
Why: eigenvalues are even; odd lives in eigenvector handedness.
If your meta norm “sees” basis mismatch, MetaΩ* rises; if you ignore U and only use Λ, it collapses to ~0.
Interpretation: Flower only “feels” spin if you include orientation-sensitive features.
C (Cloud)
MetaΩ*: moderate when you include (P^-(t)), otherwise low
Scalar diffusion invariants (trace, gap) are even → MetaΩ* small.
Transport asymmetry in kernels yields odd channel → MetaΩ* rises.
Interpretation: Cloud spin-charge is “directional transport,” not scalar defects.
R (Fractal)
MetaΩ*: near zero
Why: Fractal defects (norms, trace slopes) are conjugation-invariant scalars.
Odd channel exists only as phase orientation in log-periodic signatures, which most scalar metrics ignore.
Interpretation: Fractal is your “aether stabilizer.”
2) Pairs (2-way hybrids)
SF / FC / FR (spectral hybrids)
MetaΩ*: low if you use only Λ-type invariants; moderate if you include U-based handedness
These hybrids are built on identities that are conjugation-invariant; parity algebra is clean.
SC / CR (diffusion hybrids)
MetaΩ*: low for pure scalar defects, moderate if you include transport oddness (P^-(t))
SR (coarse-grain operator hybrid)
MetaΩ*: near zero
the SR defect is a scalar norm; parity decomposition holds exactly.
3) Triples (3-way hybrids)
SFC
MetaΩ*: low
reason: the triangle identity is backbone diagonalization; scalar defect even.
SFR
MetaΩ*: very low
reason: defects are norms + spectra; almost perfectly even.
SCR
MetaΩ*: very low
reason: coarse-grain semigroup defect is scalar even.
FCR
MetaΩ*: lowest
reason: trace identities are chirality-even and erase orientation entirely.
4) SFCR (4-way hybrid)
MetaΩ*: ~0 by design
reason: Ω*, CERT, and trace/norm-based invariants are all conjugation-invariant.
Interpretation: SFCR is the “aether gate” — it must be spin-neutral or the whole system would be biased.
5) The deep emergent understanding (why this is important)
A) Spin-charge is real structure, but it’s not allowed to corrupt the gate
Spin shows up where representation matters: Square tables, eigenvectors, transport kernels.
Spin disappears where invariants rule: trace, norms, coarse-grain defects.
That’s exactly the separation you want:
spin can exist
aether remains neutral
B) MetaΩ* tells you when you’re “using the wrong metric”
If MetaΩ* spikes in a station where you expected it low, it usually means:
your chosen norm/inner product is “seeing” orientation you intended to quotient out
or your plus/minus decomposition is being applied to a payload that isn’t truly linear in that representation
So MetaΩ* is both:
a stability signal
and a diagnostic for “metric mismatch”
6) Router guidance (simple rules)
If MetaΩ* is high at S: don’t thrash A/B; lock chirality and/or taper corridor weights.
If MetaΩ* is high at F: you’re over-emphasizing eigenvector handedness; route toward FCR/SCR where trace/defects are even.
If MetaΩ* is high at C: too much transport oddness; route to SCR (coarse-grain stabilizer) or taper W(t).
If MetaΩ* is high at SR/SFR/SCR/FCR/SFCR: that’s a bug (shouldn’t happen); it means your spin_dispatch or payload arithmetic is inconsistent.
If you want NEXT: I’ll specify the station-specific MetaΩ metric choice* (i.e., which fields to include in payload_norm) so MetaΩ* becomes meaningful and comparable across stations, rather than accidentally measuring different things in different stations.
